elements.sent-2411-018: Gamma-ray bursts have been the subject of extensive theoretical and observational interest from the community for over five decades.
elements.sent-2411-019: The duration of the gamma-ray prompt emission has lead to the separation of GRBs into long and short bursts \citep{Kouveliotou.1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Long GRBs (those with prompt emission longer than 2 seconds) were linked to the death of massive stars by the `collapsar' model \citep{Woosley.1993, MacFadyen.1999 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: This model proposes that long-GRBs are the result of a jet launched by a compact object created when a massive star undergoes core-collapse.
elements.sent-2411-022: The deceleration of this jet in the ambient medium outside of the former star is responsible for the GRB afterglow \citep{Mesz+Rees1997AG , whilst dissipation of energy within the jet powers the prompt emission \citep{Daigne.1998,Rees.1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The most notable prediction from the collapsar model is that a supernova (SN) should accompany nearly every long GRB, with the star's outer layers being accelerated by deposition of energy from the central engine within the stellar material.
elements.sent-2411-024: In 1998, the detection of GRB980425 and its associated supernova SN1998bw \citep{Galama.1998 put the collapsar model at the forefront of long GRB science.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In the years since the discovery of GRB980425-SN1998bw, over 60 GRB-SN `associations' have been detected (e.g. \cite{Finneran.2024A,Cano.201723 ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Nearly all of the supernovae associated with GRBs are broad-line Ic supernovae (Ic-BL SNe)\footnote{SN2011kl, a super-luminous, magnetar-powered SN associated with GRB111209A is the sole exception to this pattern.. .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These supernovae lack absorption features of Hydrogen and Helium in their spectra.
elements.sent-2411-028: Their photospheric spectra are dominated by broad absorption features, in particular features created by Fe, Si and Ca \citep{Cano.201723 (for reviews of SNe, see \cite{Filippenko.1997, Smartt.2009 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: These broad features are the result of the rapid expansion of the outer layers of the SN ejecta.
elements.sent-2411-030: Ic-BLs are believed to result from the death of a Wolf-Rayet star, which has been stripped of its hydrogen and helium layers, either through episodic/eruptive mass loss, mass loss due to winds, or interaction in a binary system \citep[e.g.][]{Smith.2014, Crowther.2007, Mokiem.2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The association between GRBs and Ic-BLs has been extensively investigated over the past 25 years (for a review of GRBs associated with SNe (hereafter GRB-SNe), see \cite{Cano.201723 ; a review of GRB jets in SNe may be found in \cite{Corsi.2021 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Observations of long GRBs at low redshifts indicate that the majority of these events are associated with a supernova, with simulations showing that the same central engine can power both the GRB and the Ic-BL SN \citep{Barnes.2018 .}

elements.sent-2411-063: The supernovae studied in this analysis were collected via an extensive search of the literature and online repositories, in particular the Weizmann Interactive Supernova Data Repository\footnote{\url{https://www.wiserep.org/ (WISeREP) \citep{WISEREP and the GRBSN webtool\footnote{\url{https://grbsn.watchertelescope.ie \citep{Finneran.2024A .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The data from the WISeREP archive were initially gathered for supernovae of types Ic and Ic-BL.
elements.sent-2411-065: This was intended to encompass supernovae which were perhaps labelled as a Ic prior to the common usage of the Ic-BL type in the early 2010s.
elements.sent-2411-066: This approach also ensures that supernovae which have an ambiguous type are included in the initial sample; for example, \cite{Prentice.2016 found that supernova SN2016P has an intermediate type between Ic and Ic-BL.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The initial search produced 234 Ic/Ic-BL supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-068: Transients are normally classified on WISeREP based on features visible in their early spectra.
elements.sent-2411-069: In some cases, follow-up observations around maximum light may lead to the re-classification of the transient, which is normally not reported back to WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-070: This leads to contamination of the sample with events which do not meet the criteria of a Ic-BL supernova.
elements.sent-2411-071: To confirm the classification provided by WISeREP, we followed two approaches.
elements.sent-2411-072: First, a literature search was performed for each SN.
elements.sent-2411-073: This method was used to confirm the type for 133 Ic-BL supernovae, including 63 Ic-BL supernovae which were not originally listed as Ic/Ic-BL.
elements.sent-2411-074: For those cases where no reliable classification was found in the literature, the SNe were re-classified using the Supernova Identification (SNID) tool \citep{Blondin.2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: This re-classification process was restricted to SNe with at least three publicly available spectra so that a more accurate classification could be obtained and the evolution of the SN velocity could be measured.
elements.sent-2411-076: When determining the supernova type, the redshift was fixed, as it had been measured by other analyses.
elements.sent-2411-077: Fig. \ref{fig:redshiftdist shows the redshift distribution of Ic-BL SNe with and without GRB associations considered in this study.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The age range of the SNID template spectra used for re-classification was restricted to between -10 and +45 days relative to the peak.
elements.sent-2411-079: Epochs larger than 45 days can show nebular features that are relatively similar among different SN types and can lead to uncertain classification.
elements.sent-2411-080: The -10 day restriction limits the chance that a continuum dominated template spectrum is used during the fitting.
elements.sent-2411-081: Since the default set of templates provided by SNID contains very few Ic-BL supernovae, the stripped envelope supernova templates provided by the NYU supernova group\footnote{\url{https://github.com/nyusngroup/SESNspectraLib \citep{Modjaz.2016, Liu.2016 were added to the SNID template database.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: This is intended to improve the classification accuracy for Stripped-Envelope Supernova candidates, in this case Ic-BL SNe, which otherwise can be classified as Ia SNe by SNID.
elements.sent-2411-083: This analysis ultimately revealed a further 9 Ic-BL supernovae, which as of yet have not been written up in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-084: We also confirmed the classification of 1 Ic-BL which had already been reported elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-085: Following these two approaches, a sample of 142 `verified' Ic-BL supernovae was extracted, 29 of them GRB-SNe.
elements.sent-2411-086: This sample was further reduced by imposing a minimum of three spectral observations to allow for investigation the evolution of velocity (ideally spanning a time range around the supernova peak, which is typically 10-20 days since the explosion for Ic-BLs \citep[see e.g.][]{Taddia.2019 ) and the need for a known redshift to take into account any possible cosmological effects.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The final sample consists of 61 Ic-BL supernovae and 13 GRB-SNe.
elements.sent-2411-088: A list of these SNe may be found in Table \ref{tab:sampleIcBL .}

elements.sent-2411-089: The supernova spectra, and their metadata, were downloaded from WISeREP using the WiseRep API\footnote{\url{https://pypi.org/project/wiserep-api/ \citep{wiserepapi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: This is a third party tool which was modified to provide both the spectra and their metadata from WISeREP and was also used to automate the SNID classifications.
elements.sent-2411-091: Some of the spectra were found to contain data from the same observation, uploaded multiple times to WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-092: This was common for telescopes that maintain their own database, since WISeREP downloads the spectra from these repositories as they become available.
elements.sent-2411-093: In a few cases the original observers re-uploaded these spectra following publication of the full dataset.
elements.sent-2411-094: There was also significant overlap between GRBSN Webtool data and WISeREP data.
elements.sent-2411-095: For all pairs of spectra with the same observation date, a visual examination was performed to remove duplicate spectra.
elements.sent-2411-096: This step was also used to remove any near-identical spectral pairs (for example, when galaxy-subtracted spectra were available these were retained instead of the original spectrum).
elements.sent-2411-097: If emission line removal or de-reddening of the spectrum was detected for one of the spectra in a pair, the spectrum with the highest signal to noise ratio and least evidence of artefacts was retained.
elements.sent-2411-098: The duplicate spectra flagged by this investigation are excluded from velocity measurements.
elements.sent-2411-099: In total, this study includes 875 unique spectra in the final sample analysed, which is the largest to-date for Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-100: In the case of GRB-SNe, the GRB trigger time was sourced from the GRBSN webtool and used as the explosion time ($\mathrm{t_0}$) (the GRB trigger time is typically accurate to within a few seconds).
elements.sent-2411-101: For non-GRB Ic-BLs, the explosion time needs to be calculated from the rising of their light curve using a low-order polynomial fit.
elements.sent-2411-102: Thus, the precision of $\mathrm{t_0}$ in these cases depends on how well sampled the light curve is.
elements.sent-2411-103: When available, explosion times were sourced from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-104: Otherwise, $\mathrm{t_0}$ was estimated from the light curve using publicly available photometric data.
elements.sent-2411-105: If the resulting fit produced uncertainties larger than 10 days, or in the absence of accessible photometric data, the explosion time was assumed to be the halfway point between a non-detection and the first detection, or the first detection itself depending on the available information.
elements.sent-2411-106: A visual examination was performed for all spectra.
elements.sent-2411-107: The purpose of this review was to remove corrupted data, as well as those which show nebular features or are dominated by continuum emission.
elements.sent-2411-108: Supernovae of type Ic-BL enter the nebular phase on a timescale of a few months.
elements.sent-2411-109: During this transition, the optical depth of the supernova decreases due to its continued expansion, and recombination of elements in the ejecta, rendering it transparent to optical radiation.
elements.sent-2411-110: As a result, the supernova spectrum transitions from an absorption dominated regime to an emission dominated one \citep[see e.g.][]{Sahu.2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-111: In a nebular spectrum, the minimum wavelength of absorption features may be shifted due to contamination by flux from adjacent emission lines, or the feature may disappear entirely.
elements.sent-2411-112: To avoid any contamination of the results for velocity with spectra from the nebular phase, each spectrum was examined for nebular features prior to fitting.
elements.sent-2411-113: This avoids any bias that may be introduced by filtering the spectra based on an arbitrary time when the nebular transition is expected to happen.
elements.sent-2411-114: Likewise, spectra which appear to be dominated by continuum are removed, since they have no clearly identifiable features to fit.
elements.sent-2411-115: This is often the case during the early evolution of a GRB-SN, when the contribution of the GRB afterglow is significant.
elements.sent-2411-116: Following the visual examination, an effort was made to verify the supernova redshift.
elements.sent-2411-117: This was motivated by the fact that some of the spectra available on WISeREP had already been de-redshifted.
elements.sent-2411-118: If a spectrum that has already been corrected for redshift is corrected a second time, the measured velocities will appear larger than their true values.
elements.sent-2411-119: The redshift check was usually performed by searching for the 6553$\AA$ H$\alpha$ line, and ensuring that it is centred near its rest wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-120: Since this check was performed by hand, it is only capable of catching larger discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-121: The redshift was also verified by examining the plotted spectra in the literature for some supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-122: In cases where redshift determination was not made by one of these methods, an attempt to align the spectra based on the evolution of the entire dataset was made.
elements.sent-2411-123: In this case, the velocity evolution of the full spectral sequence is comparable, even if the overall value is shifted slightly with respect to other SNe.
elements.sent-2411-124: As the supernova light propagates through its host galaxy, and the Milky Way, it is scattered and absorbed by dust grains in a process known as reddening.
elements.sent-2411-125: A correction for this reddening effect may be applied to the spectra of an SN, for example by following the prescription of \cite{CCM.1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-126: However, de-reddening typically scales the continuum of the spectrum, rather than affecting the minimum flux of absorption features, which is a function of the feature's velocity.
elements.sent-2411-127: In this analysis, no correction for reddening was performed, though it should be noted that some SNe may have been de-reddened prior to being uploaded to WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-128: Emission lines from the host galaxy or the sky, as well as cosmic ray artefacts, are present in some of the sample spectra.
elements.sent-2411-129: These may make it difficult to isolate the continuum, and can affect the location and shape of the absorption lines.
elements.sent-2411-130: Additionally, spectral smoothing is also affected by these phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-131: Since the accurate determination of the wavelength at which the spectral features reach their minimum flux is critical for this analysis, any emission lines in the spectra needed to be removed prior to the fitting of the features.
elements.sent-2411-132: Typically, emission lines are removed during the initial processing of the observational data.
elements.sent-2411-133: However, for most of the SNe in this sample, the original files are not available, and it would be impractical to perform a full data reduction analysis for such a large dataset.
elements.sent-2411-134: For this reason, an interactive interpolation-based emission line removal code, called emlineclipper\footnote{This code is freely available on Github \url{https://github.com/GabrielF98/emlineclipper , was developed.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: This method can be applied directly to the reduced one-dimensional spectra.
elements.sent-2411-136: The emlineclipper program first displays the input spectrum to the user, allowing them to choose boundaries that bracket the emission features that they wish to remove; this range is known as the `bounding region' and is demarcated by the `bounding lines'.
elements.sent-2411-137: The code then fits a spline to the data in two 100$\AA$ windows either side of the bounding region.
elements.sent-2411-138: The residuals between the spline fit and the input spectrum are computed within these windows, followed by computation of the mean and standard deviation of the residual array.
elements.sent-2411-139: These values are then used to resample the spectrum by adding noise to the spline within the bounding region.
elements.sent-2411-140: Results from emission line removal using this code are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:emclip ; the residuals are comparable in magnitude to the noise in the spectrum, and the resampled spectrum follows the evolution of the continuum near the emission lines very closely.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The program can also handle cases of multiple nearby emission lines.

Line selection is performed manually, by selecting the bounding region around the emission lines. The data in a small range either side of the emission line region are fit using a spline. The mean and standard deviation of the spline fit residuals in this region are used to resample the flux in the emission line region.} \label{fig:emclip}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Smoothing and mitigation of error sources
elements.sent-2411-142: Online repositories host spectra from a wide range of instruments and span multiple decades of observations.
elements.sent-2411-143: Thus the dataset contains spectra of varying resolutions and signal to noise ratios.
elements.sent-2411-144: In order to increase the signal to noise ratio, prior to fitting the spectra were smoothed using a Savitzky-Golay (SG) filter \citep{SG.1964 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: This increases the likelihood that the minimum wavelength is recovered for an absorption feature, especially if the input data is noisy.
elements.sent-2411-146: An SG filter has two tuneable parameters: the degree of the polynomial and the window length.
elements.sent-2411-147: A cubic polynomial was used to reduce the risk of over-fitting the data, especially near the peaks.
elements.sent-2411-148: As there is no formal method to determine the ideal window length, a statistical analysis was conducted to determine the optimum window length (Finneran et al. 2024, in prep.).
elements.sent-2411-149: This analysis suggests that the optimum window length is between 2-5\% of the spectrum length in pixels.
elements.sent-2411-150: This limit proved sufficient to avoid loss of information near the peaks when smoothing spectra in the majority of cases.
elements.sent-2411-151: Despite efforts to reduce the distortion of the spectrum during smoothing, the results are somewhat sensitive to the exact choice of smoothing parameters.
elements.sent-2411-152: Additionally the fitting of the absorption region may be sensitive to the choice of the endpoints of this region.
elements.sent-2411-153: These effects can be minimised by performing the velocity measurement for a large sample of Monte-Carlo noised spectra and averaging the result.
elements.sent-2411-154: In general, the spectral uncertainty arrays, which are required for the Monte-Carlo analysis, are not provided by online repositories.
elements.sent-2411-155: These arrays are produced during the sky-subtraction phase of spectral analysis, and cannot be reconstructed without the original data.
elements.sent-2411-156: A method to produce pseudo-uncertainty arrays is described in Appendix \ref{sec:linemethod .}
elements.sent-2411-157: One benefit of the Monte-Carlo approach is the ability to simultaneously quantify the uncertainty introduced by the signal to noise ratio of the data, the choice of the fitting region and the uncertainty introduced by the fitted function.
elements.sent-2411-158: Measuring the velocity of a spectral feature requires a high degree of confidence in the identification of the species responsible for the feature, and hence the rest-frame wavelength used during analysis.
elements.sent-2411-159: Rather than carrying out detailed modelling of the spectra for each SN, line identifications were made based on established patterns within spectra, in combination with comparison to the identifications in the literature where available.
elements.sent-2411-160: Feature identification is important for each of the three features studied here.
elements.sent-2411-161: The Fe II feature is well known to be a blend of at least three spectral lines, with rest-frame wavelengths of 4924 $\AA$, 5018 $\AA$ and 5169 $\AA$, \citep[e.g.][]{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-162: Depending on the chosen rest-frame wavelength, the Fe II velocity may differ by up to 15000 km/s once the line becomes de-blended later on in the evolution (c.f.. \cite{Prentice.2018 ).}
elements.sent-2411-163: As it is not clear which line is responsible for the minimum in any given spectrum, the 5169 $\AA$ line was chosen in order to maintain consistency with previous studies.
elements.sent-2411-164: Although \cite{Modjaz.2016 developed a method intended to counteract the effects of Fe II blending, they found it to be inapplicable to the silicon feature.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Similar methods to those used here have been applied to GRB-SNe and Ic-BLs before, with the benefit that they can be applied to all the spectral features that we wish to investigate.
elements.sent-2411-166: In cases where the iron line becomes de-blended, the line which most smoothly continues the decaying velocity trend was followed; this choice was informed by reviewing the overall spectral evolution.
elements.sent-2411-167: This avoids having a discontinuity in the velocity evolution as soon as the lines de-blend \citep[e.g.][]{Prentice.2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Applying this identification in a consistent manner to all SNe in the sample should facilitate comparison of the evolutionary trends between SNe without significant issue.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the case of the Si II feature, the canonical rest-frame wavelength is 6355 $\AA$.
elements.sent-2411-170: However, it has been proposed that blending with the Na I line may be an issue, as observed by \cite{Modjaz.2016 for PTF10qts.}

elements.sent-2411-240: For the power-law model, the function adopted was \begin{equation}\label{eq:pl} v = at^b,\end{equation} where $t$ is the rest-frame time since explosion, $a$ is the velocity of the SN one day after the explosion and $b$ is the slope of the velocity evolution; a negative $b$ value implies a velocity that decreases over time.
elements.sent-2411-241: The broken power-law adopted is similar to those used in GRB afterglow fitting \citep[e.g.][]{AMC.2014 and SN studies \citep[e.g][]{PLBPL.2017 , and can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:bpl} v = A \left[\left(\frac{t}{t_b}\right)^{-\alpha_1 s}+\left(\frac{t}{t_b}\right)^{-\alpha_2 s}\right]^{-1/s},\end{equation} where $A$ is the SN velocity at the break time, $\mathrm{\alpha_1}$ and $\mathrm{\alpha_2}$ are the slopes of the power-law segments, $\mathrm{t_b}$ is the rest-frame time of the break, and $s$ is a parameter which controls the smoothness of the break.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: While the power-law fit was performed for all SNe with at least three velocity measurements, the broken power-law fit was only performed if there were five or more velocity measurements, and only if the data showed clear deviation from the single power-law decay.
elements.sent-2411-243: An F-test was used to distinguish between power-law and broken power-law fits; requiring a three sigma confidence level improvement.

elements.sent-2411-245: A basic check for convergence, based on the autocorrelation time of the walkers was performed for each fit, as recommended by the emcee documentation.

elements.sent-2411-253: Gold sample: Fits for which the velocity evolution shows little scatter and which have converged for all parameters\footnote{For GRB-SNe the majority of the fits converged, probably because their evolution tended to be better sampled than that of the Ic-BLs; consequently the convergence criterion was ignored when determining the sample group for a GRB-SN.}

elements.sent-2411-255: Silver sample: Fits which have slightly less clear velocity evolution than the Gold sample, but which have sufficient number of datapoints to produce a good fit.
elements.sent-2411-256: The requirements on scatter and error size are relaxed compared with the Gold sample.
elements.sent-2411-257: Fits which have not converged for all parameters, but whose corner plots show good marginalisation of the posterior parameter distributions.
elements.sent-2411-258: Supernovae for which the $\mathrm{t_0}$ time is constrained via modelling or non-detections to within 5 days.
elements.sent-2411-259: Bronze sample: Fits which have a less clear velocity evolution than the Silver sample, or very few datapoints.
elements.sent-2411-260: The requirements on scatter and error size are relaxed compared with the Silver sample.

elements.subsection-2411-014: Are jets active in all Ic-BL supernovae?
elements.sent-2411-413: It appears that neither the velocity, nor its evolution are distinguishing characteristics between GRB-SNe and Ic-BL supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-414: Previous studies have ruled out GRBs (or put very stringent constraints on the phase space of the explosion) for several SNe in this sample \citep[e.g.][]{Soderberg.2006, Corsi.2016, Corsi.2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-415: Despite this, there is significant overlap between the Ic-BLs and GRB-SNe studied here, including for some which are known not to harbour a GRB.
elements.sent-2411-416: This overlap exists for all lines studied here, and therefore cannot purely be attributed to issues with line-identification or blending, since these issues do not affect every event or every feature in the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-417: Jetted emission is inferred to power the afterglow lightcurves of GRB-SNe, thus the supernovae observed in association with GRBs must contain a jet.
elements.sent-2411-418: To form a GRB the jet must have sufficient energy to break out of the stellar envelope without choking \citep[e.g.][]{Corsi.2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-419: It is thought that energy injection from a central engine may be the source of the high velocities observed in GRB-SNe.
elements.sent-2411-420: However, models of the spectra of GRB-SNe suggest that the velocity increases due to a jet are small, and are present at early times \citep{Barnes.2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-421: The results of this study suggest that jet energy injection does not have a significant influence on the velocity of the ejecta.
elements.sent-2411-422: Thus, it is not possible to conclude that jets are present in all Ic-BL SNe, or infer the frequency with which these jets may become choked and be seen as non-GRB Ic-BLs.
elements.sent-2411-423: The velocities of all three absorption features studied show evidence for a plateau phase beginning around 20 days; although for Ca II the decay in velocity is more shallow than for Fe II/Si II and the plateau is less significant.
elements.sent-2411-424: However, a study by \cite{Anand.2024 ruled out r-process enrichment in a sample of 25 Ic-BL supernovae discovered by ZTF.}
elements.sent-2411-425: Given the weight of this large sample it is difficult to conclude that the plateaus are all due to r-process elements.
elements.sent-2411-426: It is also possible that plateaus may be a natural result of the underlying ejecta's density profile.
elements.sent-2411-427: Out of all the density profiles studied in this work, only the broken power-law model shows evidence for a rapid decline in velocity followed by a slower decline phase.
elements.sent-2411-428: In this model there is an inner core where the density falls off more slowly with increasing radius, and an outer shell where the density declines more rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-429: Mixing of the radioactive nickel in the ejecta can also impact the photospheric velocity evolution and the magnitude of those velocities.
elements.sent-2411-430: They found evidence for plateaus in the photospheric velocity around 15 days prior to the bolometric luminosity peak.

elements.section-2411-007: Measuring the velocities of spectral features using splines
elements.sent-2411-480: An absorption feature is characterised by its width, depth (strength) and the wavelength at which it reaches its minimum flux.
elements.sent-2411-481: Absorption features in supernovae are generated above the photosphere, when some of the photons from the photosphere are absorbed by electrons bound within the atoms of the ejecta, forming a spectral line.
elements.sent-2411-482: The lines observed in supernovae do not appear at only one wavelength value, instead they show a broadened profile across a range of wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-483: This occurs due to the bulk motion of the particles of the gas relative to the photons.
elements.sent-2411-484: This leads to several narrow lines side by side in the spectrum, producing a broadened line.
elements.sent-2411-485: If the rest-wavelength of a spectral line can be identified, then the expansion velocity of the gas can be measured using the doppler shift formula for wavelength, \begin{equation}v = \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}\times c, \label{eq:doppler}\end{equation} where $c$ is the speed of light and $\beta$ is given by \begin{equation}\beta = \frac{\lambda^2_{obs}}{\lambda^2_{true}},\end{equation} where $\lambda_{obs}$ is the wavelength of the absorption feature measured from the spectrum, and $\lambda_{true}$ is the wavelength of the same line emitted from a gas at rest with respect to the observer.
elements.sent-2411-486: The basic tenet of this method is to fit a smooth function to the spectrum in the region of an absorption feature.
elements.sent-2411-487: The wavelength is determined from the point at which the fit reaches its minimum.
elements.sent-2411-488: This method has also been used for SESN in the past \citep{Liu.2016 , and a similar idea was used to measure the silicon 6355 $\AA$ feature for GRB130702A-SN2013dx \citep{DElia.2015 , a Ic-BL with a GRB.}}
elements.sent-2411-489: For this analysis, a method similar to those in the literature was implemented in Python.
elements.sent-2411-490: The spectrum is first smoothed using a Savitzky-Golay filter as described in Sect. \ref{sec:smoothing .}
elements.sent-2411-491: The user can adjust the smoothing window length, and a comparison of the result is displayed graphically.
elements.sent-2411-492: In order to estimate the error on the velocity, a Monte-Carlo analysis was conducted.
elements.sent-2411-493: Once the smoothed spectrum is generated, it is used to generate an uncertainty array for the analysis, in a similar manner to \cite{Liu.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-494: First, the residuals between the input and smoothed spectra are calculated.
elements.sent-2411-495: Then, a noise array is generated, where the noise at each pixel is drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero mean and standard deviation computed from the residual array.
elements.sent-2411-496: Samples from the noise array are added to the smoothed spectrum to produce 1000 mock spectra.
elements.sent-2411-497: Prior to fitting, the user selects the region of interest in the spectrum, in the case of Ic-BL spectra, the features are: Iron at 5169 $\AA$, silicon at 6355 $\AA$ and calcium at 8567 $\AA$.
elements.sent-2411-498: The red and blue boundaries of the features are selected manually on the spectrum by clicking the plot.
elements.sent-2411-499: The ultimate location of the boundaries is determined from the peaks in the smoothed mock spectra which are nearest to the user-selected points.
elements.sent-2411-500: A delta value can also be specified, which defines the region (in pixels) in which the code searches for a local maximum relative to the user's input.
elements.sent-2411-501: The value of $\delta$ is kept low, typically 30-50 pixels and is chosen so as to ensure the selection of the most appropriate local maximum for the feature in question (see Fig. \ref{fig:splinefit ).}
elements.sent-2411-502: This approach facilitates setting an approximate starting boundary, allowing the wavelength to vary based on the exact maxima in the Monte-Carlo spectra.
elements.sent-2411-503: In any case, the method is relatively insensitive to the choice of the bounding region, a conclusion which was also made by \cite{Silverman.2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-504: Once the boundaries are determined for a given spectral feature, a cubic spline is fit to each smoothed mock spectrum (see Fig. \ref{fig:splinefit ), producing a distribution of velocities.}
elements.sent-2411-505: The median velocity and the 16th and 84th percentiles are taken as the estimate for velocity and its error respectively.
elements.sent-2411-506: The cubic spline is agnostic to the shape of the complex absorption and emission processes that may shape a spectral feature.
elements.sent-2411-507: For example, the absorption in a P-Cygni profile would not produce the same feature as pure absorption would.
elements.sent-2411-508: The wavelength of the spline fit at its minimum is converted to a doppler velocity using Eq. \ref{eq:doppler .}
elements.sent-2411-509: Figure \ref{fig:splinefit shows a detailed example of this method applied to one of the SNe analysed in this paper.. \onecolumn \begin{landscape.}}
elements.sent-2411-510: In some instances, $\Delta t_{exp}$ was not known for an SN, while for the GRB-SNe $t_{exp}$ is taken from the GRB trigger time, meaning that the error is just a few seconds.
elements.sent-2411-511: For cases where the SN type is ambiguous, the second-most likely type is shown.
elements.sent-2411-512: The number of spectra for each SN is also shown; with the number in brackets indicates the number of these spectra that show evidence of nebular features.

elements.sent-2411-540: Broad-lined type Ic (type Ic-BL) supernovae (SNe) are an observationally rare class of supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-541: Photometrically these events are among the brightest supernovae, reaching peak absolute magnitudes of around -18.5 mag after 10-15 days \citep{Taddia.2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-542: Spectroscopically they are distinguished by the absence of hydrogen and helium absorption features and presence of broad absorption features of Fe, Si and Ca in their photospheric spectra (for reviews of SN classifications see \cite{HandbookSNeGalYam.2017,Filippenko.1997 ).}
elements.sent-2411-543: These broad features are the result of the rapid expansion of the outer layers of the SN ejecta, with typical ejecta velocities being in the $\sim$15000-20000\,km/s range at peak light \citep{Srinivasaragavan.2024,Taddia.2019,Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-544: Type Ic-BL SNe are believed to result from the death of a Wolf-Rayet star, which has been stripped of its hydrogen and helium layers, either through episodic/eruptive mass loss, mass loss due to winds, or interaction in a binary system \citep[e.g.][]{Smith.2014, Crowther.2007, Mokiem.2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-545: Gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) are brief, intense pulses of gamma-rays and have been the subject of extensive theoretical and observational interest from the astronomy community for over five decades.
elements.sent-2411-546: This type of SN is known as an engine-driven SN.
elements.sent-2411-547: In 1998, the detection of GRB980425 and its associated broad-lined type Ic supernova (type Ic-BL SN) SN1998bw put the collapsar model at the forefront of long GRB science \citep{Galama.1998 .}
elements.sent-2411-548: In the years since the discovery of GRB980425-SN1998bw, more than 60 `GRB-SN associations' have been detected (e.g. \cite{Finneran.2024A,Cano.201723 ).}
elements.sent-2411-549: Nearly all of the supernovae associated with GRBs are type Ic-BL SNe\footnote{SN2011kl, a super-luminous, magnetar-powered SN associated with GRB111209A is the sole exception to this pattern \citep{Greiner.2015 .. .}}

elements.sent-2411-584: The abundance of spectral data in online repositories allows for a quantitative investigation of velocity evolution in these supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-585: This paper investigates the spectral velocity evolution of the largest sample of type Ic-BL SNe and GRB-SNe collected to date, consisting of almost 900 spectra.
elements.sent-2411-586: A spline fitting method is adopted to measure the velocities of the Fe II and Si II absorption features within the type Ic-BL SN spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-587: The focus of the investigation is to determine whether the velocities of these events can help us to distinguish between Ic-BL SNe with and without GRBs.
elements.sent-2411-588: Section \ref{sec:sample describes the collection of sample data for this research, highlighting some of the challenges involved in the collation of a large heterogeneous dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-589: The supernovae studied in this analysis were identified via an extensive search of the literature and online repositories.
elements.sent-2411-590: In particular, we made extensive use of the Weizmann Interactive Supernova Data Repository\footnote{\url{https://www.wiserep.org/ (WISeREP) \citep{WISEREP and the GRBSN webtool\footnote{\url{https://grbsn.watchertelescope.ie \citep{Finneran.2024A .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-591: Our sample selection process is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:samplenumnbers .}
elements.sent-2411-592: We began by searching WISeREP for supernovae of types Ic and Ic-BL with at least 3 spectral epochs and measured redshift.
elements.sent-2411-593: This allowed us to include supernovae that may have been labelled as type Ic SNe prior to the common usage of the Ic-BL SN type in the early 2010s.
elements.sent-2411-594: This approach also ensures that supernovae which have an ambiguous type are included in the initial sample.
elements.sent-2411-595: For example, \cite{Gangopadhyay.2020 mention that SN2016P has spectral features whose widths are in between type Ic and type Ic-BL SNe, while \citet{Prentice.2019 say that its velocities are more akin to type Ic SNe.}}
elements.sent-2411-596: We also cross-checked the list of type Ic-BL SNe provided by WISeREP with those on the Transient Name Server\footnote{\url{tns-wis.org (TNS).}}
elements.sent-2411-597: This search is believed to be complete up to early April 2024.
elements.sent-2411-598: The initial WISeREP search produced 8 GRB-SNe, 130 type Ic and 44 type Ic-BL supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-599: However, transient classifications reported on WISeREP may not be accurate, as they tend to be based on very early observations, often from only a single spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-600: Later observations may alter the classification of the SN, but this information may not be updated on WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-601: This could lead to contamination of our sample with events which do not meet the criteria of a type Ic/Ic-BL supernova.
elements.sent-2411-602: This method was used to confirm the SN type of 8 GRB-SNe and 43 type Ic-BL supernovae.
elements.sent-2411-603: For those SNe where no reliable classification could be found in the literature, we classified the SNe using the Supernova Identification (SNID) tool \citep{Blondin.2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-604: To improve the classification accuracy of SNID for Ic-BL SNe, we included the stripped-envelope supernova (SESN) templates provided by the NYU supernova group\footnote{\url{https://github.com/nyusngroup/SESNspectraLib \citep{Modjaz.2016, Liu.2016 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-605: During classification, we set the SN redshift to the value published on WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-606: The age range of the SNID template spectra used for classification was restricted to $t_{peak}$-10 and $t_{peak}$+45 days.
elements.sent-2411-607: Epochs larger than 45 days can show emission features that are relatively similar among different SN types and can lead to uncertain classification.
elements.sent-2411-608: This analysis identified 9 type Ic-BL SNe which (as of April 2024) have not yet been published.
elements.sent-2411-609: The literature search and SNID classification of our WISeREP list resulted in a confirmed sample of 8 GRB-SNe and 52 type Ic-BL SNe.
elements.sent-2411-610: A separate literature search for type Ic-BL SNe identified an additional GRB-SN and 61 type Ic-BL SNe.
elements.sent-2411-611: These had not been identified in the initial WISeREP search because they were not listed as type Ic or Ic-BL SNe.
elements.sent-2411-612: A search of the GRBSN webtool turned up 29 GRB-SNe, 9 of which had already been found in the literature or on WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-613: Combining events found in multiple data sources, at this point the sample consisted of 113 ordinary type Ic-BL SNe and 29 GRB-SNe.
elements.sent-2411-614: This sample was further reduced by imposing a minimum of three spectral observations to allow for investigation of velocity evolution, and the need for a pre-determined redshift to take into account any possible cosmological effects.
elements.sent-2411-615: The final sample consists of 61 type Ic-BL SNe without observed GRBs and 13 GRB-SNe.
elements.sent-2411-616: The ordinary type Ic-BL supernovae presented in this study were generally discovered by magnitude limited surveys such as the ZTF Bright Transient Survey \citep{Fremling.2020 , which conducts a magnitude limited survey down to $m_{peak \leq$ 18.5 mag.}}
elements.sent-2411-617: This results in the sample containing a larger fraction of bright supernovae than exists in reality within the volume sampled by the magnitude limit.
elements.sent-2411-618: This magnitude cut is made more severe by the requirement for these SNe to have spectroscopic confirmation (which is more likely for brighter SNe) and for there to be several spectra with sufficient signal-to-noise ratio to measure a velocity (which requires either a brighter SN or a longer observation/larger telescope).
elements.sent-2411-619: There are some benefits to the abundance of bright supernovae in the sample, in that it is often easier to fit the early lightcurve of bright supernovae to determine their $t_0$, since there are likely more observations from surveys earlier in their evolution.
elements.sent-2411-620: This can help to mitigate the issues around uncertainty on $t_0$, including its influence on parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-621: Another aspect of including an excess of bright SNe is that the brightness of a type Ic-BL supernova is directly related to the amount of nickel produced in the explosion \citep{Arnett.1982 .}
elements.sent-2411-622: In GRB-associated SNe, simulations have shown that much of this Nickel production takes place on the jet-axis \citep{Shankar.2021 , possibly making the supernova brighter along this axis.}
elements.sent-2411-623: Consequently, an over-abundance of bright, survey detected SNe might bias the sample towards type Ic-BL SNe with a polar viewing angle.
elements.sent-2411-624: This has implications for the interpretation of an absence of a detectable GRB jet in many of these explosions.
elements.sent-2411-625: Figure \ref{fig:redshiftdist shows the redshift distributions of of ordinary type Ic-BL SNe and GRB-SNe.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Ordinary type Ic-BL SNe are detected up to $z\sim$0.2, while GRB-SNe range in redshift up to $z\sim$0.6.
elements.sent-2411-627: This is a result of differences in how these events are discovered.
elements.sent-2411-628: Ordinary type Ic-BL SNe tend to be discovered in magnitude limited surveys, which imposes a redshift limit on the SNe that can be detected.
elements.sent-2411-629: In contrast, GRB-SNe are normally discovered during optical follow-up of a GRB trigger.
elements.sent-2411-630: Knowing where and when to look for the supernova allows us to detect type Ic-BL SNe associated with GRBs at greater distances than those SNe detected by surveys.
elements.sent-2411-631: With the differences in redshift distributions of GRB-SNe and ordinary type Ic-BL SNe comes the potential for differences in the environmental conditions of these events, in particular metalicity.
elements.sent-2411-632: However, several studies indicate that the environments of long-GRBs and type Ic-BL supernovae are very similar \citep{Sanders.2012a,Japelj.2018,Modjaz.2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-633: As a result, the difference in redshift distributions is not expected to significantly effect any conclusions drawn here.
elements.sent-2411-634: The impact of redshift on the measured spectral velocities has been accounted for by de-redshifting each spectrum before computing the velocity.

elements.sent-2411-635: The supernova spectra and metadata were downloaded from WISeREP and the GRBSN webtool.
elements.sent-2411-636: We also downloaded spectra for GRB180427A-SN2018fip from the ESO Archive\footnote{\url{https://archive.eso.org/cms.html .}}
elements.sent-2411-637: Spectra from WISeREP were downloaded using the WiseRep API\footnote{\url{https://pypi.org/project/wiserep-api/ \citep{wiserepapi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-638: This is a third party tool which was modified to provide both the spectra and their metadata from WISeREP and was also used to partially automate the SNID classifications discussed previously.
elements.sent-2411-639: Several instances of duplicate spectra were found in the data downloaded from WISeREP.
elements.sent-2411-640: This was common for telescopes that maintain their own database.
elements.sent-2411-641: WISeREP automatically uploads new spectra from these repositories.
elements.sent-2411-642: In some cases the original observers re-uploaded these spectra following publication of the full dataset, resulting in duplicate spectra.
elements.sent-2411-643: There was also significant overlap between GRBSN Webtool data and WISeREP data, because both tools contain data sourced from the supernova literature.
elements.sent-2411-644: To deal with duplicate and similar spectra, a visual examination was performed for all pairs of spectra with the same observation date.
elements.sent-2411-645: This step was also used to remove any near-identical spectral pairs.
elements.sent-2411-646: For example, when galaxy-subtracted spectra were available these were retained instead of the original spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-647: We found 232 duplicate spectra via this search.
elements.sent-2411-648: These spectra were excluded from velocity measurements.
elements.sent-2411-649: The final sample studied here includes 875 unique spectra, which is the largest to-date for type Ic-BL SNe.
elements.sent-2411-650: In the case of GRB-SNe, the GRB trigger time was sourced from the GRBSN webtool and used as the explosion time (${t_0}$) (the GRB trigger time is typically accurate to within a few seconds).
elements.sent-2411-651: For non-GRB type Ic-BL SNe, the explosion time needs to be calculated from the rising of their light curve using a low-order polynomial fit.
elements.sent-2411-652: Thus, the precision of ${t_0}$ in these cases depends on how well sampled the light curve is.
elements.sent-2411-653: Otherwise, ${t_0}$ was estimated from the light curve using publicly available photometric data.
elements.sent-2411-654: The supernovae analysed in this work span a wide range of redshifts.
elements.sent-2411-655: The expansion of the universe causes spectral features formed in the SN frame to appear at lower velocities in the observer frame.
elements.sent-2411-656: To account for this effect, we divide the observed wavelengths of our spectra by $1+z$, where $z$ is the redshift of the source.
elements.sent-2411-657: To perform this correction we use the reported redshift on WISeREP/GRBSN webtool.
elements.sent-2411-658: We initially assumed that the downloaded spectra had not been corrected for redshift, however we then noticed that some spectra from these sources had already undergone redshift correction.
elements.sent-2411-659: If a spectrum that has already been redshift-corrected is corrected a second time, our measured velocities would appear larger than their true values.
elements.sent-2411-660: To try to avoid this issue we performed a visual examination of each spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-661: When we found evidence of prior redshift-correction, we flagged this spectrum to ensure that the correction was not applied a second time.
elements.sent-2411-662: One marker we used to determine whether a spectrum had already been redshift-corrected was the 6563\,\AA\,\,H$\alpha$ host-galaxy line.
elements.sent-2411-663: This line was chosen because it can be easily identified, and several of our SNe exhibit this line ($\sim$40\%, though we note that it may have been removed from some spectra prior to their publication).
elements.sent-2411-664: If this line was centred at its rest-wavelength in the downloaded spectrum, then the spectrum has already been corrected for redshift.
elements.sent-2411-665: Since this check was performed visually, it is only capable of catching larger discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-666: In cases where there was no H$\alpha$ line from the SN host, we compared our redshift-corrected spectrum with the redshift-corrected spectrum in the published literature.
elements.sent-2411-667: When easily identified features were similarly positioned in both the published spectrum and our redshift-corrected spectrum, we considered our spectrum to have been properly redshift-corrected.
elements.sent-2411-668: In some cases we were unable to verify whether or not redshift corrections had already been applied to a spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-669: This often happened due to a lack of obvious emission features or because there were no publications available as a cross-check.
elements.sent-2411-670: In these instances we applied the redshift correction ourselves before performing a visual examination of the spectral sequence.
elements.sent-2411-671: With this examination we ensured that similar spectral features remain aligned across the dataset throughout the spectral evolution.
elements.sent-2411-672: This allowed us to unearth some instances in which spectral features were shifted with respect to the same features in the rest of the spectral sequence.
elements.sent-2411-673: This generally indicates that this spectrum had already been redshift-corrected.
elements.sent-2411-674: As the supernova light propagates through its host galaxy and the Milky Way, it is scattered and absorbed by dust grains in a process known as reddening.
elements.sent-2411-675: Note that we have not explicitly removed telluric features from our spectra.
elements.sent-2411-676: These absorption features are likely not an issue in the Fe II region of the spectrum but may contribute to the Si II feature.
elements.sent-2411-677: In some cases these lines will have been dealt with during the spectral reduction.
elements.sent-2411-678: The code then fits a spline to the data in two 100\,\AA\,\,windows either side of the bounding region.
elements.sent-2411-679: An SG filter has two tunable parameters: the degree of the polynomial and the window length.
elements.sent-2411-680: As there is no formal method to determine the ideal window length, a statistical analysis was conducted to determine the optimum window length \citep{Finneran.2024C .}
elements.sent-2411-681: This analysis suggests that the optimum window length is between 2-5\% of the spectrum length in resolution elements.
elements.sent-2411-682: The fine-tuning of smoothing parameters in this method typically introduces an additional uncertainty of $\sim$500-1000\,km/s \citep{Finneran.2024C .}
elements.sent-2411-683: Feature identification is important for each of the three features that dominate the type Ic-BL spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-684: The Fe II feature is well known to be a blend of at least three spectral lines, with rest-frame wavelengths of 4924\,\AA, 5018\,\AA\,and 5169\,\AA, \citep[e.g.][]{Modjaz.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-685: As it is not clear which line is responsible for the minimum in any given spectrum, the 5169\,\AA\,line was chosen in order to maintain consistency with previous studies.
elements.sent-2411-686: Although \cite{Modjaz.2016 developed a method intended to counteract the effects of Fe II blending, they found it to be inapplicable to the Si II feature.}
elements.sent-2411-687: Similar methods to those used here have been applied to GRB-SNe and ordinary type Ic-BL SNe before, with the benefit that they can be applied to all the spectral features that we wish to investigate.
elements.sent-2411-688: In cases where the Fe II feature becomes de-blended, the line which most smoothly continues the decaying velocity trend was followed; this choice was informed by reviewing the overall spectral evolution.
elements.sent-2411-689: In the case of the Si II feature, the canonical rest-frame wavelength is 6355\,\AA.
elements.sent-2411-690: There is also an ongoing debate as to whether the feature near 6100\,\AA, commonly identified with Si II, is in fact entirely due to silicon, or whether it is contaminated by other elements including H$\alpha$ in absorption \citep{Parrent.2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-691: For Ic-BL SNe the source of this hydrogen would likely be in the material expelled by the WR progenitor star.
elements.sent-2411-692: The Ca II feature is also a triplet, consisting of lines at 8498\,\AA, 8542\,\AA, and 8662\,\AA\,\citep[e.g.][]{Rho.2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-693: Following \cite{Rho.2021 , a mean wavelength of 8567\,\AA\,was used as the rest-wavelength for this feature.}
elements.sent-2411-694: Indeed, as this triplet is in the near-infrared range of the spectrum it has rarely been studied for a large population of type Ic-BL SNe.
elements.sent-2411-695: Another complication with the Ca II feature is its proximity to the O I 7774\,\AA\,feature, with which it may be blended.
elements.sent-2411-696: Although we attempted to fit this feature, we were not able to obtain robust fits and thus omit these fits from the present analysis.

elements.sent-2411-747: The observation of visually similar decay-index distributions motivated the use of a KS-test to validate the null-hypothesis that these decay indices are drawn from the same underlying distribution.
elements.sent-2411-748: This test was performed using the scipy Python library \citep{SciPy , using the ks\_2samp function \citep[method is based on][]{Hodges.KSTest .}}
elements.sent-2411-749: A two-sample KS-test compares the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of two discrete empirically observed distributions, computing the maximum difference between the two CDFs.
elements.sent-2411-750: This statistic is then compared to the KS distribution to determine the $p$-value.
elements.sent-2411-751: In this test, the null-hypothesis is typically that the two distributions are drawn from the same population, with the alternative hypothesis being that the distributions are drawn from distinct populations.
elements.sent-2411-752: In this work, we reject the null-hypothesis if $p<0.01$.

elements.sent-2411-770: We also considered the possibility that a large uncertainty in $t_0$ measurements for our type Ic-BL SN sample may have been responsible for our observation of broken power-law evolution.
elements.sent-2411-771: This is because shifting the velocity data in time may introduce conditions under which power-law evolution may be best fit (erroneously) by a broken power-law.
elements.sent-2411-772: We quantified this using simulated data from a broken power-law, and then artificially shifting this data in time and re-fitting it using power-law and broken power-law fits.
elements.sent-2411-773: We then determined the best-fit using an F-test.
elements.sent-2411-774: We did this for multiple shifts of $t_0$ (from -6 to +10 days) and concluded that errors in the fit parameters grow as uncertainty in $t_0$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-775: However, we also found that no amount of uncertainty on $t_0$ can cause a power-law fit to become the preferred fit compared to a broken power-law fit and vice-versa.
elements.sent-2411-776: It is based on these conclusions that we decided to present statistical tests of our measured parameters using only the Gold and Silver samples, which have well-constrained $t_0$.
elements.sent-2411-777: We present our full analysis in Appendix \ref{sec:testrobustness .}

elements.sent-2411-946: If the rest-wavelength of a spectral line can be identified, then the expansion velocity of the gas can be measured using the Doppler shift formula for wavelength, \begin{equation}v = \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}\times c, \label{eq:doppler}\end{equation} where $c$ is the speed of light and $\beta$ is given by \begin{equation}\beta = \frac{\lambda^2_{obs}}{\lambda^2_{true}},\end{equation} where $\lambda_{obs}$ is the wavelength of the absorption feature measured from the spectrum, and $\lambda_{true}$ is the wavelength of the same line emitted from a gas at rest with respect to the observer.

elements.section-2411-012: How robust are broken power-law fits to uncertainties in explosion time?
elements.sent-2411-954: The broken power-law fits presented in this paper were motivated by the observation of apparent breaks in the slope of the velocity evolution for both Si II and Fe II, and their adoption as the best-fit to the observations is supported by the results of an F-test when compared to a power-law fit.
elements.sent-2411-955: However, it is possible that the model parameters, and potentially the best-fitting model, may change if the supernova explosion epoch, $t_0$, is uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-956: This is not the case for GRB-SNe, as their $t_0$ is very well constrained by the trigger time of gamma-ray observing satellites derived from the prompt gamma-ray emission.
elements.sent-2411-957: However, for the majority of the ordinary type Ic-BL supernovae in this sample, there is some uncertainty in $t_0$, and the level of uncertainty is not uniform across the sample.
elements.sent-2411-958: It is therefore important to understand the impact that uncertainty in $t_0$ may have on the marginalised parameters of the models.
elements.figure-2411-067: \caption[Model parameters used to generate a simulated dataset]{Model parameters chosen to generate a simulated dataset used in verifying the robustness of the broken power-law fits. These values are the average best-fit values for the velocity evolution of the Si II feature in ordinary Ic-BL supernovae.} \label{tab:simdataparams} \begin{tabular}{l c l} Parameter Value Unit $A$ 15000 km/s $\alpha_1$ -0.35 $\alpha_2$ -1.4 $t_b$ 20 days $s$ 20 days \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-068: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figures/exampledata.pdf} \caption[Simulated broken power-law and synthetic data]{Simulated broken power-law and synthetic data used to test robustness of broken-power law fits. The circular points have been sampled from the broken power-law, represented by the solid line. The parameters of the broken power-law can be found in Table \ref{tab:simdataparams}. A 500 km/s Gaussian scatter has been added to the sample data points to make the data more realistic.}\label{fig:syntheticdata}
elements.figure-2411-070: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/Aviolins.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/alpha1violins.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/alpha2violins.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/tbviolins.pdf} \caption[Violin plots showing the distributions of broken power-law parameters for a given shift in $t_0$]{Violin plots showing distributions of broken power-law parameters for a given shift in $t_0$. The dotted lines show the simulated values of these parameters for a given $t_0$ shift. All values are subject to variation due to these $t_0$ shifts. Top: Distributions for the Si II velocity at the break, $A$. Second from top: Distributions for the pre-break power-law index, $\alpha_1$. Second from bottom: Distributions for the post-break power-law index, $\alpha_2$. Bottom: Distributions for the break time, $t_b$.}\label{fig:t0testviolins}
elements.sent-2411-959: To estimate the impact of this source of uncertainty, a simulated dataset was created using the broken power-law given in equation \ref{eq:bpl .}
elements.sent-2411-960: Table \ref{tab:simdataparams shows the model parameters used to generate the simulated data.}
elements.sent-2411-961: These values are the average best-fit values for the velocity evolution of the Si II feature in ordinary type Ic-BL supernovae (all of these supernovae are in the Gold sample).
elements.sent-2411-962: Figure \ref{fig:syntheticdata shows the broken power-law model and a sample of 7 data points taken from this curve between $t_0$+8 and $t_0$+32 days with regular spacing.}
elements.sent-2411-963: A 500 km/s Gaussian scatter was added to make the data more representative of real observations.
elements.sent-2411-964: From this simulated dataset, multiple datasets were generated in which the data were shifted along the time axis by between -6 and +10 days (in increments of 1 day).
elements.sent-2411-965: This simulates the potential uncertainty on the $t_0$ value, with a shift of -1 day representing a true $t_0$ that occurs 1 day after the $t_0$ of the non-shifted data-set.
elements.sent-2411-966: These datasets were then fit using the same MCMC fitting procedure used to compute velocity evolution parameters in previous analyses.
elements.sent-2411-967: Both power-law and broken power-law models were fit to these datasets, and an F-test was then used to determine which model was the best fit.
elements.sent-2411-968: Based on a 2-$\sigma$ confidence level, the $p$-values of the F-tests indicate that this range of uncertainty in $t_0$ does not alter the best-fit from a broken power-law to a power-law.
elements.sent-2411-969: This result suggests that the observation of broken power-laws is robust.
elements.sent-2411-970: The parameters arising from this analysis are shown in Table \ref{tab:t0testparams .}
elements.sent-2411-971: The parameters presented in Table \ref{tab:t0testparams show that although the observation of broken power-laws is robust, some of the parameters of the best-fit broken power-law are subject to significant variance under changes in $t_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-972: Figure \ref{fig:t0testviolins shows how the best-fit parameters and their distributions are altered as $t_0$ is shifted.}
elements.sent-2411-973: This figure shows that $A$ is consistently over-estimated and $t_b$ consistently under-estimated compared to their values used when generating the model dataset, no matter the $t_0$ shift applied.
elements.sent-2411-974: This may have been caused by the introduction of Gaussian noise to the simulated dataset.
elements.sent-2411-975: Table \ref{tab:t0testparams confirms that the values for a time-shift of 0 days are consistent within their uncertainties with those of the parameters used to generate this data.}
elements.sent-2411-976: The value of $A$ is robust to changes in $t_0$, and the value of $t_b$ changes as expected when the data are shifted in time.
elements.sent-2411-977: However, the values of the power-law indices are not robust to shifting the $t_0$.
elements.sent-2411-978: Both $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are reduced (relative to the 0-day shift value) when data are shifted forward in time, i.e. the power-laws become steeper.
elements.sent-2411-979: The opposite is true when the data are shifted backwards in time.
elements.sent-2411-980: This effect is more pronounced for $\alpha_2$.
elements.sent-2411-981: However, there is no evidence that $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ converge towards each other, which would lead to a power-law becoming the best-fit.
elements.sent-2411-982: This is in line with the results of the F-tests.
elements.sent-2411-983: It can be inferred that if data generated from a power-law was used in such analysis, a similar change in $b$ (the power-law index) would result.
elements.sent-2411-984: This has implications for all conclusions drawn from the power-law indices used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-985: It is for this reason that statistical tests used to draw conclusions from quantities dependent on the power-law index are performed using only the Gold sample, which has the smallest uncertainty on $t_0$.
elements.sent-2411-986: The GRB-SNe are not impacted by this effect, because they have highly constrained $t_0$ values.. \FloatBarrier.
